critique.summary: The article describes the author's personal experience migrating various digital services and subscriptions from non-EU providers to EU-based alternatives, motivated by political concerns and the EU's strong data protection laws. The author details their choices for email, calendar, web hosting, domain registration, Git hosting, VPN, and devices, emphasizing privacy and data sovereignty.
mainPoints: The author prefers EU service providers due to better data protection and political reasons.
Fastmail was the previous email provider, replaced by mailbox.org and then Uberspace for better domain and sender address flexibility.
NextCloud was installed on Uberspace to provide calendar and file sharing functionality.
The website hosting was migrated from a Hetzner VPS to Uberspace, adapting to Apache from Nginx.
Domain registration moved from Namecheap to hosting.de, a German provider.
Git repositories were moved from GitHub and self-hosted Gitea to codeberg.org, a German nonprofit.
VPN service remains Mullvad, a Swedish no-logs provider.
The author uses a Pixel 9a with Graphene OS and alternative app stores to reduce Google dependency, and plans to replace a Chromebook with a MacBook Air running MX Linux.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the EU having strong data protection laws align with the widely recognized General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). The described service providers such as mailbox.org, Uberspace, NextCloud, hosting.de, codeberg.org, and Mullvad are legitimate and known entities with the stated features. The technical details about email sending capabilities, calendar solutions, and hosting environments are consistent with publicly available information. The personal opinions about service quality and usability are subjective but plausible. The article does not cite external sources or provide multiple perspectives, as it is a personal account rather than investigative journalism.
considerations: The article reflects a single user's experience and preferences, which may not generalize to all users.
Costs, performance, and feature trade-offs between providers are not deeply analyzed or compared with data.
Potential challenges or limitations of the EU providers, such as pricing or service availability, are only briefly mentioned.
The impact of switching providers on data migration, continuity, and user experience is not fully explored.
The political reasons for migration are mentioned but not elaborated, limiting understanding of that motivation.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around migrating tech infrastructure and services to the EU, with users sharing experiences about hosting, email providers, and regulatory concerns. Some highlight benefits like data sovereignty and competitive hosting prices, while others warn about EU legal overreach and political instability.

comments.author: pschastain
comments.text:
How comfortable are you guys with the fact that EU countries allow prosecutors and sometimes even police officers to issue their own search warrants without meaningful judicial review? Some EU courts will not exclude illegally obtained evidence either, so challenging the warrant later on will be pointless.

Oh, and you might be in a reasonable EU country and still be hit with an EIO from one of the unreasonable countries. This is especially concerning given recent ECJ rulings increasingly directing courts in receiving nations to blindly defer to the requesting party when dealing with EAWs, EIOs and similar.

Worth considering when hosting in the EU.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a nuanced legal perspective on EU law enforcement powers and their implications, which is well-known and relevant for data hosting decisions.

comments.author: kouunji
comments.text: Honestly this is part of a macro trend of everyone outside the US scrambling to get off a US tech stack…these are going to be the longer term economic consequences for the country, as it is no longer seen as a safe option for any kind of data or service exposure.
comments.analysis: The comment accurately identifies a growing trend of non-US entities seeking alternatives due to privacy and sovereignty concerns, reflecting broader geopolitical shifts.

comments.author: _pdp_
comments.text: Our company started migrating our tech stack from USA to EU. We are about 90% there with a few small dependencies that could be resolved but we have not yet tackled.
comments.analysis: A practical firsthand report of migration progress, adding real-world context to the discussion.

comments.author: s_dev
comments.text:
https://european-alternatives.eu/

I recommend Scaleway for cloud hosting. I recently migrated from Digital Ocean who I really loved, to Scaleway and have I have to say impressed with both dashboard interface and pricing so far.

In work we still use AWS but everything is hosted in eu-west (Ireland) in AWS EU Sovereign cloud but not sure how truly compliant this is in a CloudAct vs GDPR showdown.

I've yet to migrate from namecheap but planning on moving my domains to inwx. My MacBook Pro will be hard to replace so that will be years away. Nothing phones look cool but I would like to go with EU solutions rather than British ones. https://commerce.jolla.com/products/jolla-phone-sep-ii-2026 looks cool but some the HackerNews guys have been quite critical so I'm still considering what those next devices will be.
comments.analysis: Provides useful recommendations and highlights compliance uncertainties, contributing valuable practical insight.

comments.author: appstorelottery
comments.text: I would add Hetzner for hosting. German based, solid in my experience with virtual servers.
comments.analysis: A straightforward endorsement of a reputable EU hosting provider, useful for readers considering migration.

comments.author: _joel
comments.text: You can take fastmail from my cold, dead hands :D About the only thing I can rely on to actually work.
comments.analysis: Expresses strong personal loyalty to Fastmail, a known reliable email provider, though subjective and less informative.

comments.author: deaux
comments.text: https://bunny.net/ seems solid as a Cloudflare and S3 replacement. I'm not affiliated but they deserve more mentions in these threads.
comments.analysis: Offers a useful alternative service recommendation, adding value to the discussion.

comments.author: dragochat
comments.text:
how about the OPPOSITE problem: _anyone knows of any non-EU AND non-US email providers_? with email accounts as the roots of trust for many things, i'd really wanna know how can I get a trustworthy one not-attached to eithern an unstable system (US), or a very overregulating one like the EU juristictions...

and ofc, non-CN too
comments.analysis: Raises a valid concern about finding trustworthy email providers outside dominant jurisdictions, encouraging further discussion.

comments.author: pennaMan
comments.text:
> the EU currently has the most user-friendly laws when it comes to data protection

This is laughable. The EU has the most big-tech regulatory capture friendly data laws that make it really hard for small companies to compete, nicely packaged under consumer protection pretenses.

Those same laws give the institutions of the state complete and total right to silently wiretap the digital existence of anyone, at any time, for any reason.
comments.analysis: Critiques EU data laws from a skeptical perspective; while somewhat emotional, it highlights real concerns about regulatory complexity and surveillance.

comments.author: aborsy
comments.text: Some of these European countries such as France are quite authoritarian. They frequently pass (update: propose/push for) laws to ban VPN and even social media, request access to private messages, etc. It seems to me the situation is equally bad in EU.
comments.analysis: Provides a critical view of authoritarian tendencies in parts of the EU, which is supported by recent legislative trends.
